comments.text: Since Erdős offered 1000 rupees for both the first and the second question (ie 2000 in total), unless they're separated into distinct problems, arguably this problem is worth \$78 instead of \$39. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

As my note solves a problem for which Erdős offered a small reward, I decided to send it to the online journal Integers to make it a bit more official. I owe a big thanks to Terence for helping me, both with valuable feedback on the mathematical content, as well as getting the paper reviewed in the first place. One can use the following reference now: W. van Doorn, On the length of an interval that contains distinct multiples of the first $n$ positive integers. Integers 26 (2026), #A7. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Remarkably, it seems that this problem (morally, at least) implies the Kakeya conjecture in all dimensions! Proof sketch: if the Kakeya conjecture failed, then by the tensor power trick we conclude that for sufficiently high dimension $d$, there exists Kakeya sets of dimension less than $d-1$. Discretizing (and taking some liberties with the analogy between arithmetic progressions and tubes), we get about $N^{d-1}$ arithmetic progressions of spacing $\sim N$ that occupy less than $N^{d-1}$ elements, so it is not possible to assign each such progression a distinct element. Morally, one can then invoke the Chinese remainder theorem to conclude.
Interesting! The 1000 rupees prize seems rather undervalued then... Does the 3/2 exponent of Erdos and Pomerance translate to anything for Kakeya using this argument?

It corresponds to 3D Kakeya sets having dimension at least 2, which can be established by elementary incidence geometry (mostly using the fact that two lines intersect in at most one point). This suggests that the sums-differences approach of Bourgain (I also worked on this with Nets Katz) to Kakeya in high dimensions may make progress on this 3/2 exponent. This article of Green and Ruzsa may be a starting point for anyone wanting to pursue this. (Incidentally, Conjecture 5 from that paper relates to a problem of Erdos and Selfridge - is it already on this site?)
